coredata.dc:title: Computationally engineering optoelectronic properties and photocatalytic performance in graphene quantum dot/platinum(II) complex photocatalysts
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Wu, Qiulan
coredata.dc:description: This study delves into the impact of various substituents on bipyridyl Pt(II) bisstilbenylacetylide complexes when attached to Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a material with promising photocatalytic potential. Using density functional theory calculations, the research uncovers that the substituents significantly alter properties of the resulting nanocomposites. These include the geometric and electronic structures, optical absorption profiles, charge transfer configurations, and chemical reactivity. Electron-withdrawing groups cause blueshifts in absorption peaks, while electron-donating ones induce redshifts. These shifts are more pronounced when the complexes are attached to GQDs. The attachment of GQDs also impacts the molecular orbital structure, stabilizing the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), but destabilizing the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Furthermore, the incorporation of GQDs into the composite enhances overall stability while diminishing reactivity. The formation of the nanocomposite reduces the energetic favorability of electron injection and regeneration processes. Interestingly, the substituents affect the charge transfer length more significantly for nanocomposites compared to isolated complexes. Overall, this study provides valuable insights into fine-tuning optoelectronic and photocatalytic properties of GQD hybrid photocatalysts, useful in applications like solar energy conversion and pollutant degradation. These findings significantly advance the understanding of the structure–function relationship in GQD nanocomposites for photocatalysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of structure of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite. R represents the auxiliary substituent attached to the 4-ethynylstilbene ligand. R = NO2, CHO, Br, H, OCH3, NPh2.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite, as well as the isolated Pt(II) complex. In this diagram, the red dashed line represents the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2, while the black dashed line represents the HOMO of the I-/I3- redox pair.
(a)-(f) Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite.
schematic illustration of interfacial charge transfer dynamics in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite.
(a)-(f) Absorption spectra of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites.

Electron and hole distribution of first and main absorption peaks of (a,b) gqd/pt(ii):no2, (c,d) GQD/Pt(II):CHO, (e,f) GQD/Pt(II):Br, (g,h) GQD/Pt(II):H, (i,j) GQD/Pt(II):OCH3 and (k,l) GQD/Pt(II):NPh2.
Chemical reactivity parameters and charge transfer length (ΔL) in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite.
Free energies of electron injection (ΔGinj) and ground-state regeneration (ΔGreg).
Energy conversion efficiency (Ƞ) and related photovoltaic parameters.

head.ce:title: Computationally engineering optoelectronic properties and photocatalytic performance in graphene quantum dot/platinum(II) complex photocatalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study delves into the impact of various substituents on bipyridyl Pt(II) bisstilbenylacetylide complexes when attached to Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a material with promising photocatalytic potential. Using density functional theory calculations, the research uncovers that the substituents significantly alter properties of the resulting nanocomposites. These include the geometric and electronic structures, optical absorption profiles, charge transfer configurations, and chemical reactivity. Electron-withdrawing groups cause blueshifts in absorption peaks, while electron-donating ones induce redshifts. These shifts are more pronounced when the complexes are attached to GQDs. The attachment of GQDs also impacts the molecular orbital structure, stabilizing the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), but destabilizing the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Furthermore, the incorporation of GQDs into the composite enhances overall stability while diminishing reactivity. The formation of the nanocomposite reduces the energetic favorability of electron injection and regeneration processes. Interestingly, the substituents affect the charge transfer length more significantly for nanocomposites compared to isolated complexes. Overall, this study provides valuable insights into fine-tuning optoelectronic and photocatalytic properties of GQD hybrid photocatalysts, useful in applications like solar energy conversion and pollutant degradation. These findings significantly advance the understanding of the structure–function relationship in GQD nanocomposites for photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the field of photocatalysis, dyes are mainly used as photosensitizers in many applications due to their highly efficient light absorption properties and consequent enabling of effective energy transfer in semiconductor photocatalysts, which characteristically exhibit impressive activity levels [1–3]. Other reasons for the increasing attractiveness of dye complexes in dye-based catalysts against organic pollutants are their high stability and longer lifetime, which allows complete degradation in long-term reactions [4,5]. From these advantages, it can be concluded that the use of dye complexes can both generate clean energy and combat pollution from organic pollutants. For example, according to dye studies by Schott et al [6], a particularly suitable complex such as bisamide ruthenium polypyridyl is characterized by excellent activity and improved stable photosensitization in the generation of hydrogen compared to other complexes. Also worth mentioning are noble metal-based photocatalysts such as Pt(II) complexes, which were highlighted in the work of Wu et al [9] as excellent, clean, and life-sustaining solar options for organic conversion during photocatalysis. The forthcoming extension to more sustainable and efficient processes in photocatalysis is therefore an exciting prospect. The incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into dye complexes is further evidence of efforts to explore all potential avenues for improving the photocatalytic properties of nanocomposites upon visible light irradiation [7,8]. Ultimately, the results of these advanced studies only underscore that GQDs have excellent potential to improve the photocatalytic properties of nanocomposites.

When it comes to performing photochemistry catalytically, the use of Pt(II) complexes offers several advantages. They exhibit superior longevity, making them ideal candidates for continuous use in attrition-free conditions [9]. Their strong light absorption capabilities, combined with efficient energy transfer capacity, lead to enhanced photoactivity in semiconductor catalysis. Recent research by Li et al. [10] demonstrates how noble metals like Pt can enhance O2 evolution as co-catalysts over various semiconductors. This study reveals that Pt plays a crucial role in enhancing O2 adsorption on photocatalysts, resulting in improved degradation activity. Wu et al. [11] indicate that metal complexes, such as Pt(II), possess favorable light absorption abilities, which are ideal for initiating organic transformations effectively during photocatalytic processes. In particular, Platinum diimine bis(acetylide) complexes have been extensively studied due to their unique properties and potential applications in various fields. These complexes exhibit excellent stability and electron-donating/accepting abilities, making them suitable as triplet photosensitizers for photocatalysis [12,13]. Extensive investigation has been conducted on their luminescence properties, exploring the nature of their excited states through ligand variation [12]. Additionally, their electrochemical, photophysical, and photochemical properties have been explored, revealing broad emission spectra and high emission quantum yields [14]. Platinum diimine bis(acetylide) complexes show promise in various applications, including photocatalysis [15,16], photoinduced charge separation [17,18], organic light-emitting devices (OLEDs) [19,20], upconversion [21,22], and nonlinear optics [16,23]. Their unique properties and potential applications have made them the subject of extensive research [24]. The photophysical properties of Platinum diimine bis(acetylide) complexes can be tuned by varying the substituents on the 4-ethynylstilbene ligand since the triplet excited state resides on these ligands [7]. Therefore, the ground and excited state properties are highly sensitive to the auxiliary substituents. However, despite extensive studies and their use in photocatalysis, the effect of auxiliary substituents on the photophysical properties of platinum diimine bis(acetylide) complexes remains largely underdeveloped. Given the promising application potential of these types of compounds, further research on platinum diimine bis(acetylide) complexes is highly recommended.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising components in photocatalytic systems due to their unique properties [25,26]. Firstly, GQDs possess a wide absorption range and high extinction coefficient, enabling efficient light harvesting [27,28]. Recent studies have demonstrated promising practical uses of GQD-based photocatalysts. By absorbing light and transferring the energy to the co-sensitizer, GQDs extend its light absorption capabilities and enhance the generation of reactive species for photocatalysis. For instance, Tsai and Hsu showed CdSe nanocrystals coupled with GQDs exhibited enhanced photoelectrochemical hydrogen production compared to CdSe alone [29]. Chang et al. incorporated GQDs into CdSe-sensitized TiO2 nanorods to improve solar hydrogen generation [30]. Additionally, Cui and Xue computationally analyzed a GQD/Ir(III) complex nanocomposite for photocatalytic pollutant degradation [7]. These works reveal how GQDs can optimize the photocatalytic performance of various systems spanning pollutant remediation to solar fuel synthesis. Secondly, GQDs act as electron donors or acceptors, facilitating charge transfer between the Pt(II) complex and the semiconductor [31–35]. This promotes the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, minimizing charge recombination, and improving photocatalytic efficiency. Thirdly, the small size and high surface area of GQDs enable a strong interaction with the Pt(II) complex, enhancing energy and electron transfer processes. Lastly, the excellent stability and biocompatibility of GQDs make them suitable for practical applications of the Pt(II)-GQDs system in photocatalysis. By harnessing the synergistic effects of GQDs and Pt(II) complexes, enhanced photocatalytic performance can be achieved, leading to advancements in various photocatalytic applications.

This study aims to investigate the impact of auxiliary substituents on the charge transfer configurations and the resulting enhancement in the photocatalytic performance of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The bipyridyl Pt(II) bisstilbenylacetylide complexes serve as the key components in the nanocomposites, offering unique electronic properties and structural features that facilitate efficient charge transfer processes. By systematically varying the auxiliary substituents, we will explore the different charge transfer configurations enabled by these modifications. The interplay between the auxiliary substituents and the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite structure will be investigated to elucidate the mechanisms underlying the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The findings from this study will not only contribute to the fundamental understanding of charge transfer processes in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites but also offer valuable guidance for the design and development of highly efficient photocatalytic materials. Ultimately, these advancements can pave the way for the utilization of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites in various applications, including solar energy conversion, pollutant degradation, and sustainable synthesis processes.

The initial geometry of the Pt(II) complex nanocomposite is obtained from experimental structures [16], as shown in Scheme 1 . The GQD is attached to the Pt(II) complex through amide bonding to form the nanocomposite. In order to facilitate the identification of the nanocomposite, we have named these nanocomposites using the format GQD/Pt(II) complex:R. The letter 'R' represents an auxiliary substituent attached to the end of the 4-ethynylstilbene ligand. The possible options for R include NO2, CHO, Br, H, OCH3, or Naphthyl (NPh2). These substituents vary in their electron-withdrawing ability, with NO2 being the strongest, followed by CHO, Br, H, NPh2 and OCH3. The last two substituents are electron-donating groups. Therefore, in total, we have constructed six different GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites, namely GQD/Pt(II):NO2, GQD/Pt(II):CHO, GQD/Pt(II):Br, GQD/Pt(II):H, GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 and GQD/Pt(II):OCH3. Moreover, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) rely on the efficient interaction between the dye sensitizers and the TiO2 semiconductor surface. The choice of the TiO2 surface is crucial for achieving high DSSC efficiency, with the (101) surface of bulk anatase TiO2 being particularly favored due to its low surface energy [34,35]. To understand the charge transfer dynamics in these systems, small cluster models of TiO2 have been employed, such as the (TiO2)38 cluster [7,36]. This cluster model accurately captures the intricate charge transfer processes between the dye sensitizers and TiO2, making it a valuable tool in research studies. Experimental validation of hybrid systems involving (TiO2)38 clusters has shown good agreement with the calculated optoelectronic properties [37]. In this study, the (TiO2)38 cluster model was utilized to represent the semiconductor surface in six hybrid systems comprising GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites and (TiO2)38. The Pt(II) complexes were bound to the (TiO2)38 surface via a bidentate chelating mode, ensuring stable interaction. Consistent with previous findings, the calculated Ti-O bond lengths (2.16–2.19 Å) in the molecular systems were in line with experimental observations [38–42]. This comprehensive approach enables a detailed understanding of the charge transfer dynamics and the structural characteristics of the hybrid systems, contributing to the development of efficient DSSCs.

The DFT calculations are employed to optimize the geometry of the nanocomposite to determine the most stable and energetically favorable configuration. Convergence criteria are applied during the optimization process, which include energy and gradient thresholds. Specifically, the maximum and root mean square (RMS) forces are set at 0.00045 Hartree/Bohr and 0.0003 Hartree/Bohr, respectively. Additionally, the maximum and RMS displacements are limited to 0.0018 Bohr and 0.0012 Bohr, respectively. These criteria ensure the attainment of a well-converged and reliable geometry for the nanocomposite [41]. The B3LYP hybrid density functionals have been found to be effective for studying transition metal-containing systems, including metal complexes and nanocomposites [7,42]. This is because the functional balances the accuracy of exchange and correlation, enabling reliable and precise calculations. Additionally, the LANL2DZ/6–31 g(d,p) mixed basis set is commonly used for transition metal-containing systems [42–46], making it a suitable choice for the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite. The basis set is specifically designed to simulate core electrons and valence electrons separately, with the LANL2DZ portion handling the core electrons, and the 6–31 g(d,p) basis set accounting for the valence electrons. This mixed basis set can adequately capture the electron density of the metallic centers without the need for excessive computational resources. Previous benchmarking on similar systems showed B3LYP/LANL2DZ/6–31 g(d,p) provides good agreement with experiments for transition metal complexes and nanocomposites [45–51]. Our test calculations of spectral parameters matched literature data [16], validating the accuracy. The extensive prior use of B3LYP/LANL2DZ/6–31 g(d,p) for related complexes [50–54] and nanomaterials [48,51,53,54] also supports its application here. This combined rationale provides strong justification for using B3LYP/LANL2DZ/6–31 g(d,p) to reliably and efficiently model the GQD/Pt(II) nanocomposite while maintaining accuracy. Therefore, using the B3LYP functional and LANL2DZ/6–31 g(d,p) mixed basis set could provide an accurate and efficient means for studying the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite.

The optimized geometry of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite is then used to calculate the excited electronic states using time-dependent DFT (TDDFT), which is implemented in the Orca software package [55,56]. The number of excited states required for the simulation of the absorption spectrum is 40. The absorption spectrum can be calculated based on the oscillator strength or transition dipole moment between the ground and excited states. Geometric structure optimization was performed without considering the effect of spin–orbit coupling (SOC). Since the ligand field environment of the transition metal complex has minimal impact on SOC, this simplification allowed a focus on the geometry of the system while disregarding SOC effects. For the single-point calculation, a scalar relativistic Self-Consistent Field (SCF) method was used. The SCF method incorporated the Douglas-Kroll-Hess (DKH) Hamiltonian, which accounts for relativistic effects in electronic property calculations. This choice of method was made to accurately describe the system's electronic behavior, considering relativistic effects. Specifically, the relativistically recontracted def2-TZVPP basis set and effective core potentials were utilized for the Pt and Ti atoms, while the def2-svp basis set was employed for the other atoms involved in the calculations.

Furthermore, a charge transfer analysis was conducted to explore the extent of charge transfer and the distances between fragments within the system. The charge transfer distance (ΔL) and transferred charges between fragments were quantified, using the multiwfn software [57]. This analysis provided valuable insights into the charge transfer dynamics and interactions within the nanocomposite.

The optimization process revealed that the ground singlet state of these complexes has an essentially planar geometry. In this geometry, the stilbenyl components of the 4-ethynylstilbene ligands were found to be twisted relative to the platinum (Pt) coordination plane by approximately 1°. The substituent groups, on the other hand, remained in the stilbenyl plane except for the NPh2 group, which adopted a propeller-like shape. Furthermore, the analysis showed that the average Pt-N (platinum–nitrogen) bond length was 2.087 Å (angstroms) and was not affected by the presence of 4-ethynylstilbene substituent group. In contrast, the Pt-C (platinum–carbon) bond lengths were found to be about 1.940 Å and were slightly influenced by the substituent groups. This implies that the substituent groups had minimal impact on the overall bond lengths and geometric structure of these complexes. The Pt-C bonds exhibit greater substituent effects because of their direct connection to the substituents, π-bonding character allowing electronic influences, conformational flexibility to accommodate steric factors, and inherent sensitivity to the chemical environment, whereas the Pt-N bonds are rigidly held in a chelate ring with strong σ-bonding character, restricting geometric variations and electronic effects. Essentially, the bonding factors of the Pt-C bonds, including their connectivity, electronic character, flexibility, and intrinsic sensitivity, make them more prone to substituent impacts than the isolated, electronically-insensitive, and conformationally-rigid Pt-N bonds. The computational results agree with these fundamental bonding differences that cause the Pt-C lengths to be more affected by substituents than the Pt-N lengths.

Fig. 1 provides valuable insights into the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) in Pt(II) complexes and their nanocomposites. The LUMOs of these molecular systems exhibit higher energy levels compared to the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2, measured at −4.0 eV [7,8]. On the other hand, the HOMOs of these systems vary, with some being higher and others lower than the HOMO (-4.8 eV [7,8]) of the redox pair I-/I3-. It is noteworthy that most of the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites have lower HOMO levels than the HOMO of I-/I3-. However, one particular nanocomposite, GQD/Pt(II):NPh2, shares the same HOMO level as I-/I3-, due to that the NPh2 group introduces electron-donating properties to the nanocomposite, resulting in a higher HOMO energy level. Similarly, among the isolated Pt(II) complexes, only Pt(II):NO2, Pt(II):CHO, and Pt(II):Br exhibit lower HOMO levels than the HOMO of I-/I3-. This difference can be attributed to the distinct electronic properties introduced by the respective auxiliary substituents (NO2, CHO, and Br) attached to the 4-ethynyl-stilbene ligand. These substituents possess electron-withdrawing characteristics, thereby reducing the electron density around the Pt(II) complex and lowering the HOMO energy level. Conversely, the remaining Pt(II) complexes, which lack lower HOMO levels than the HOMO of I-/I3-, likely have auxiliary substituents with neutral or electron-donating nature, resulting in comparable or higher HOMO energy levels. The significance of these findings lies in the efficient energy conversion potential exhibited by the Pt(II) complexes and GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites. The higher LUMO levels than the CBM of TiO2 facilitate effective electron transfer [7,8]. The HOMO energy level affects the ground state regeneration step, where the oxidized dye catalyst must be reduced back to the ground state by accepting an electron from a redox species. A higher, more positive HOMO energy level is disadvantageous for regeneration because it reduces the thermodynamic driving force for hole transfer from the HOMO to the redox species. The lower driving force results in slower regeneration kinetics and increased time spent in the oxidized excited state. In contrast, a lower, more negative HOMO energy level is advantageous because it provides greater thermodynamic driving force for hole transfer to the redox species. This allows faster ground state regeneration to complete the catalytic cycle. Therefore, in terms of regeneration kinetics, a lower HOMO energy level is preferred as it enables faster recovery of the ground state. However, the HOMO must still be high enough to match the redox potential for the process to be feasible.

The difference in HOMO levels between nanocomposites and isolated Pt(II) complexes indicates redistribution of charges in GQD/Pt(II) nanocomposites with auxiliary groups that either donate or weakly withdraw electrons. The NPh2 group demonstrates stronger electron-donating characteristics compared to OCH3, Br, and H. Hence, when NPh2 is attached to GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite, it readily donates its electrons to the GQD matrix, resulting in a more pronounced stabilization of the HOMO level [34]. The OCH3 group has electron delocalization compared to Br and H, primarily due to oxygen's electronegativity relative to both bromine and hydrogen. This higher electronegativity facilitates the donation of electron density. These delocalized electrons readily interact with the GQD, leading to a stabilization of the HOMO level. Additionally, the O and N atoms in OCH3 and NPh2 possess lone pair electrons that do not participate in bonding but interact with the GQD. Conversely, hydrogen lacks pairs, which contributes to the observed trend in HOMO stabilization. Moreover, even though bromine weakly withdraws electrons, it still has an electron-drawing effect on the complex. This effect limits the spread of electrons in the GQD, leading to a stabilization of the occupied molecular orbital HOMO in comparison to NPh2.

Meanwhile, the PDOS confirms that the Pt(II) complex states govern the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the nanocomposite. The Pt(II) complex plays a crucial role in managing the initial excitation and subsequent movement of photo-generated charge carriers, thereby influencing the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite. The Pt(II) complex acts as a 'charge trap', reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs due to its slower electron-hole recombination rate (typically in the microseconds range [42]) compared to GQDs (typically in the nanoseconds range [58]). This feature contributes to a more efficient photocatalytic performance. Additionally, the Pt(II) active sites provide pathways for necessary photocatalytic redox reactions, enhancing light utilization and charge transfer, resulting in improved photocatalytic reactions. The presence of both GQDs and the Pt(II) complex combines the benefits of reduced recombination rates (provided by the Pt(II) complex) and a reservoir of energy states and facilitation of charge transfer (provided by GQDs), both of which are essential for photocatalytic functions.

To investigate the influence of different auxiliary groups on the light absorption of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites, we computed the absorption spectra using the TDDFT approach. To verify the accuracy of the chosen functional and basis set, we calculated the energies of the first optically active peak of pristine GQD and the main absorption peaks of Pt(II) complexes, comparing the calculated results with earlier studies. The calculated excitation energy of pristine GQD is 3.70 eV, which is in good agreement with the tight binding calculation results [59]. Moreover, the calculated excitation energies for Pt(II) complex functionalized with electron-withdrawing groups follow the trend Pt(II):NO2 (2.41 eV) < Pt(II):CHO (2.74 eV) < Pt(II):Br (3.13 eV), while for Pt(II) complex passivated with electron-donating groups, the trend is Pt(II):OCH3 (2.95 eV) > Pt(II):NPh2 (2.81 eV). All main absorption peaks are red-shifted compared to that of Pt(II):H (3.18 eV), which agrees with experimental observations [42]. Furthermore, the variation trend of excitation energy with respect to the electron-withdrawing and -donating abilities of the auxiliary group also matches the experimental results [42], confirming the validity of our computational algorithms. This trend suggests that increasing the electron-withdrawing or decreasing the electron-donating ability of the auxiliary group results in a red-shift of the main absorption peak of the Pt(II) complex. Overall, increasing the electron-withdrawing or decreasing the electron-donating strength of the auxiliaries leads to a red-shift of the main absorption peaks for the Pt(II) complexes. This is due to the destabilization of the HOMO or the stabilization of LUMO levels through inductive or resonance effects, resulting in a decrease in the HOMO-LUMO gap and excitation energy.

The initial peaks (S1) of GQD/Pt(II):NO2, GQD/Pt(II):CHO, and GQD/Pt(II):Br absorb light at different wavelengths compared to GQD/Pt(II):H. However, the initial peaks of GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 and GQD/Pt(II):OCH3 also absorb light at different wavelengths. The absorption intensity of the peaks decreases as the auxiliary groups decrease their ability to withdraw electrons (Fig. 4 (a)-(c)). However, it is not significantly affected by the auxiliary groups' ability to donate electrons (Fig. 4 (e)-(f)). When the electron-withdrawing auxiliary groups are stronger, they stabilize the Pt(II) complex's HOMO, leading to a larger energy gap between the HOMO and the LUMO. This results in a shift of the absorption peak and higher absorption intensity due to the reduced nonradiative recombination. On the other hand, electron-donor auxiliary groups destabilize the HOMO more than the LUMO [60], leading to a reduction of energy gap and a red shift in the absorption peaks. The similar intensities of these peaks suggest that the degree of stabilization is less dependent on the strength of electron donor groups. For the absorption peak (Smax), reducing the electron-withdrawing ability of the group decreases its absorption intensity. Decreasing the electron donor ability of the group primarily affects the position of the Smax, causing it to shift towards shorter wavelengths (blue shift).

In general, the presence of an electron-withdrawing auxiliary greatly affects both the location and intensity of S1 as well as the intensity of Smax in the nanocomposite. On the other hand, an electron-donor auxiliary mainly influences the location of S1 and Smax in the nanocomposite.

Fig. 5 displays the electron-hole distribution maps for the S1 and Smax of GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites. In the maps, electrons are represented by the color green, and holes are represented by the color blue. These maps help identify the physical origins behind the spectral shifts. To offer a more detailed insight into the charge distributions, we can supplement it with supplemental Figure S1, which lists the percentages of electron and hole densities on each fragment of the nanocomposite for various excitations. It is clear that the first absorption peaks are governed by the Pt(II) complex, featured with ligand-to-ligand charge transfer (LLCT), intraligand charge transfer (ILCT), metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT), and π,π* transition from the acetylide ligand. The main absorption peaks are featured with ILCT, π,π* transition from GQD, intermolecular charge transfer from GQD to Pt(II) complex, π,π* transition from the acetylide ligand, and LLCT. The attachment of the GQD to the Pt(II) complex significantly impacts its Smax through a multitude of changes arising from the interaction. These changes include orbital mixing between the frontier orbitals, enabling electrons to delocalize over the entire nanocomposite, thus affecting accessible electronic transitions and the absorption spectrum. Additionally, there are additional intermolecular charge transfer processes enabled by the presence of the GQD, involving both components contributing to the main absorption peak, as noted in the electron-hole distribution maps. Other modifications include changes in the HOMO and LUMO levels of the Pt(II) complex relative to its isolated state, altering the energy gaps available for electronic excitation, which impacts the absorption peak position and intensity. Furthermore, there are new optical selection rules within the overall asymmetric nanocomposite system, governing which electronic transitions are allowed and affecting the absorption profile. Lastly, there may be potential conformational changes induced in the Pt(II) complex by the GQD, altering its available electronic transitions and absorption characteristics. The complex interplay between these diverse factors underlies the observed photophysical behavior of these hybrid nanocomposites.

The charge transfer length (ΔL) significantly impacts the photocatalytic performance of the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite. A longer charge transfer length allows for efficient charge separation, transport, and utilization of carriers for catalytic reactions. It minimizes recombination, promotes charge migration to Pt(II) complex sites, and improves the interaction between GQDs and Pt(II) complexes. Factors such as interfacial properties, band structure alignment, and defects influence the charge transfer length and should be optimized to maximize photocatalytic performance. Table 1 demonstrates how the electron-withdrawing or electron-donating nature of the auxiliary group can affect ΔL. Specifically, stronger electron-withdrawing groups tend to decrease ΔL, while electron-donating groups can increase it. For instance, the GQD/Pt(II) nanocomposite with the strong electron-withdrawing NO2 group exhibits a relatively shorter charge transfer length of 5.21 Ã… (Å). On the other hand, the GQD/Pt(II) nanocomposite with the electron-donating NPh2 group shows a longer charge transfer length of 13.7 Å. Furthermore, among Pt(II) complexes, the charge transfer length (ΔL) exhibits a moderate variation with different auxiliary groups. For example, comparing Pt(II) complexes with NO2 and NPh2, the charge transfer length changes from 6.20 Å to 9.76 Å. This indicates that the auxiliary group influences the charge transfer length to some extent in Pt(II) complexes, although the effect is relatively modest. On the other hand, the introduction of the auxiliary group in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites appears to have a more pronounced impact on the charge transfer length. Comparing GQD/Pt(II) nanocomposites with NO2 and NPh2, the charge transfer length changes from 5.21 Å to 13.7 Å. This indicates a relatively larger variation in the charge transfer length for the nanocomposites compared to the Pt(II) complexes alone. The enhanced sensitivity of the charge transfer length to the auxiliary group in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites can be attributed to the unique properties and interactions of the GQDs with the Pt(II) complexes. The presence of GQDs introduces additional pathways and potential electronic coupling, which can facilitate or hinder charge transfer processes. Consequently, the auxiliary group's electron-withdrawing or electron-donating nature can exert a more significant influence on the charge transfer length within the nanocomposite compared to the Pt(II) complexes alone.
Chemical hardness is a measure of a molecule's resistance to changes in electron density.

Higher values of chemical hardness indicate greater stability and lower reactivity. According to Table 1, nanocomposites generally exhibit higher chemical hardness values compared to the corresponding Pt(II) complexes. This suggests that the introduction of GQDs enhances the stability and decreases the reactivity of the resulting nanocomposites.

The chemical potential represents the energy required to add or remove an electron from a system. A more negative value of the chemical potential indicates a greater tendency to accept electrons [61]. The electrophilicity index reflects the ability of a molecule to act as an electron acceptor. Higher electrophilicity values indicate a stronger tendency to accept electrons. When comparing the changes in chemical potential between the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites and Pt(II) complexes, the following trends are observed:

The nanocomposites generally exhibit less negative (i.e., higher) chemical potentials compared to their corresponding Pt(II) complexes for GQD/Pt(II):NO2, GQD/Pt(II):CHO, and GQD/Pt(II):Br. However, the opposite is true for GQD/Pt(II):H, GQD/Pt(II):NPh2, and GQD/Pt(II):OCH3. This indicates that the attachment of GQD to these complexes (GQD/Pt(II):NO2, GQD/Pt(II):CHO, and GQD/Pt(II):Br) results in a higher electron-donating tendency or a lower energy requirement for electron removal. However, for GQD/Pt(II):H, GQD/Pt(II):NPh2, and GQD/Pt(II):OCH3, the chemical potentials of the nanocomposites are more negative (lower) than those of their corresponding Pt(II) complexes, suggesting a higher electron-accepting tendency or a higher energy requirement for electron removal. Furthermore, when examining the electrophilicity indices (γ), the nanocomposites generally have smaller values compared to their corresponding Pt(II) complexes. This suggests that the nanocomposites are relatively less electron-deficient or more electron-rich compared to the Pt(II) complexes. The smaller electrophilicity indices indicate a higher tendency for the nanocomposites to donate electrons or engage in nucleophilic reactions.

The observations seem contradictory because they suggest conflicting trends between the chemical potentials and electrophilicity indices of the nanocomposites compared to their corresponding Pt(II) complexes. According to the first observation, for GQD/Pt(II):H, GQD/Pt(II):NPh2, and GQD/Pt(II):OCH3, the chemical potentials of the nanocomposites are more negative (lower) than those of their corresponding Pt(II) complexes. This implies a higher electron-accepting tendency or a higher energy requirement for electron removal in the nanocomposites. However, the second observation states that when examining the electrophilicity indices, the nanocomposites generally have smaller values compared to their corresponding Pt(II) complexes. This suggests that the nanocomposites are relatively less electron-deficient or more electron-rich compared to the Pt(II) complexes, indicating a higher tendency to donate electrons or engage in nucleophilic reactions.

These insights highlight the influence of GQD attachment on the chemical potentials and electrophilicity indices of the nanocomposites compared to their corresponding Pt(II) complexes. The presence of GQD introduces electronic interactions and alterations in the charge distribution, leading to variations in the electron-donating or electron-accepting characteristics of the nanocomposites. In the nanocomposites where the chemical potentials are more negative (lower) compared to the Pt(II) complexes, suggesting a higher electron-accepting tendency, one possible explanation is the electron-withdrawing effect of GQD. GQD may have vacant electron-deficient orbitals or π* antibonding orbitals that can interact with the Pt(II) complex, resulting in charge redistribution and a higher affinity for accepting electrons. This electron-withdrawing effect can lead to a more negative chemical potential. On the other hand, the smaller electrophilicity indices in the nanocomposites, indicating a greater tendency to donate electrons, may stem from the electron-donating effect of the Pt(II) complex. The electron-donating auxiliary groups may possess electron-rich π orbitals or lone pairs of electrons that can donate electron density to the Pt(II) complex, leading to an increase in the electron density and a greater likelihood of electron donation. This electron-donating effect can result in a smaller electrophilicity index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Driving forces of electron injection and ground-state regeneration
ce:section.ce:para: The driving forces of electron injection (ΔGinj) and ground-state regeneration (ΔGreg), which refer to the free energies of those electron transfer processes, are important determinants of the photocatalytic activity for several reasons. More negative (more favorable) ΔGinj and ΔGreg values can enable faster electron transfer kinetics, promote greater separation and stabilization of charges after injection, and reduce overpotential losses. All of these factors tend to accelerate the catalytic cycle, improve the photochemical yields, and boost the catalytic efficiency. The calculation details of ΔGinj and ΔGreg can be found in previous works [7,8].

The values in Table 2 indicate different extents of variation in ΔGinj depending on the auxiliary group. Specifically, Pt(II):NO2 and Pt(II):OCH3 show relatively similar ΔGinj values, while Pt(II):CHO and Pt(II):Br display lower values. Among the Pt(II) complexes, Pt(II):H and Pt(II):NPh2 exhibit the smallest ΔGinj values, suggesting a more favorable driving force for electron injection in these two complexes. Meanwhile, Pt(II):NO2 and Pt(II):CHO demonstrate comparable ΔGreg values, while Pt(II):H and Pt(II):Br show slightly higher values. Additionally, Pt(II):OCH3 has a relatively higher ΔGreg value, and Pt(II):NPh2 exhibits the highest value among the Pt(II) complexes. The variation of ΔGinj and ΔGreg indicates that increasing the electron-withdrawing ability of the auxiliary group reduces the driving force of electron injection but enhances the ground-state regeneration ability of the Pt(II) complex. Conversely, increasing the electron-donating ability of the auxiliary group enhances the driving force of electron injection while reducing ground-state regeneration. These trends in forces of electron injection and ground-state regeneration arise from electron-withdrawing groups that draw electrons from the pendant phenyl ring, resulting in a relatively more positive and nucleophilic environment. This facilitates electron donation from the redox pair to the ground state during regeneration, leading to a more negative ΔGreg. Moreover, stabilizing the positive charge on the metal center by electron-withdrawing groups reduces the energy required for oxidation and results in a less negative ΔGinj.

Incorporating the GQD component into the Pt(II) complexes introduces further variation in the values of ΔGinj and ΔGreg. The presence of GQD generally leads to an increase in ΔGinj values compared to the respective Pt(II) complexes without GQD. Among the nanocomposites, GQD/Pt(II):H and GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 show the most significant increase in ΔGinj. The ΔGreg values of the nanocomposites also exhibit an increase compared to the respective Pt(II) complexes without GQD, indicating reduced ground-state regeneration capabilities. This reduction in electron injection and ground-state regeneration forces is likely due to the perturbation of the electronic properties of both GQD and Pt(II) complexes upon coupling. These perturbations could disrupt the previously optimized electronic structures for injection and regeneration, making both processes less favorable. Moreover, the interfacial interactions between GQD and Pt(II) complexes may stabilize the charge-separated states involved in electron transfer (i.e., after injection or before regeneration). This extra stabilization would decrease the driving force for both processes, resulting in a less negative free energy. Additionally, coupling the two components may introduce new molecular motions or reorganizations at the interface, thereby increasing the reorganizational energy of electron transfer. This additional energy barrier would contribute to less negative ΔGinj and ΔGreg values.
Overall, attaching GQD to Pt(II) complexes results in a reduction in the driving forces of electron injection and ground-state regeneration.

The energy conversion efficiency (Ƞ) plays a significant role in determining the photocatalytic performance of a nanocomposite. It is closely tied to the nanocomposite's ability to convert light energy into useful chemical reactions. Several factors influence the efficiency, including the nanocomposite's optical properties, charge separation and transfer efficiency, and catalytic activity. Optimal optical properties enable efficient light absorption within the desired wavelength range, initiating the photocatalytic process. Effective charge separation and transfer prevent recombination, maximizing the number of available charge carriers for catalytic reactions. Additionally, high catalytic activity, influenced by surface area, morphology, and composition, enhances the nanocomposite's ability to utilize photo-generated charge carriers. By optimizing these factors, the energy conversion efficiency can be improved, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance characterized by faster reaction rates and higher yields.

The η is determined by various factors, including the open circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current (Jsc), and fill factors (FF). This relationship can be quantified using the equation [7,8]:
(1)
η =
V
oc
J
sc
F F
P
in
Here, Pin represents the input power or irradiance. The calculation details of Voc, Jsc and FF can be found in supplemental materials.

The association between Voc and electron lifetime (tex) is quite close, as tex has a considerable impact on the effectiveness of charge gathering. When the lifetime of electrons is longer, there is an improved ability to collect electrons at the electrode before recombination takes place. This leads to a reduction in recombination losses and an overall increase in the efficiency of the solar cell. As a result, a longer electron lifetime typically yields a higher open-circuit voltage. The Jsc of a solar cell is determined by several factors. Firstly, the light collection efficiency (LHE), representing the number of photons per unit area, time, and wavelength, has a direct relationship with Jsc. Higher LHE results in increased photon absorption and more charge carriers generated, leading to a higher Jsc. Additionally, the time it takes for charge carriers to be injected (tinj), relax within the material (trelax), travel to the collection point (ttrans), and recombine or lose their energy (trec) also influence Jsc. A shorter tinj and ttrans, along with longer trelax, allow for faster charge carrier generation and collection, contributing to a higher Jsc. Conversely, a shorter trec can lead to more recombination and decrease Jsc. By optimizing these parameters, the short-circuit current of a solar cell can be maximized, enhancing its overall performance.

According to Marcus theory, non-radiative electron transfer refers to the process in which energy exchange between electronic states occurs without emitting or absorbing light. This transfer is influenced by three factors: the free energy change (ΔG), reorganization energy (λ), and electronic coupling (V). The ΔG represents the difference in free energy between the initial and final states, determining the thermodynamic feasibility of the transfer. λ quantifies the energy required to rearrange the environment for electron transfer, reflecting the flexibility and polarizability of the molecules involved. V measures the strength of interaction between electron-donating and electron-accepting entities, influencing the probability of electron transfer. Table 3 presents the electron injection times for different GQD/Pt(II) complexes: GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 (0.69 fs) < GQD/Pt(II):H (0.94 fs) < GQD/Pt(II):CHO (3.06 fs) < GQD/Pt(II):OCH3 (3.49 fs) < GQD/Pt(II):Br (3.73 fs) < GQD/Pt(II):NO2 (4.33 fs). The electron injection time is consistent with the free energy change of electron injection listed in Table 2, except for GQD/Pt(II):NO2. This indicates that the free energy change plays a dominant role in regulating the electron injection in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites, influencing the probability of electron transfer. In the case of GQD/Pt(II):NO2, the driving force of electron injection is greater than that of GQD/Pt(II):OCH3 and GQD/Pt(II):Br, despite the slower electron injection time in GQD/Pt(II): NO2. Upon closer inspection of the LUMO orbital of Pt(II):NO2 in the GQD/Pt(II):NO2@TiO2 nanohybrid system (supplemental Table S1), it is suggested that the LUMO of Pt(II):NO2 is spread over the 2,2′-bipyridine and 4-ethynyl-stilbene ligands. On the other hand, the LUMOs of the other two Pt(II) complexes are localized in the benzene ring attached to the carboxylate group. This localization benefits the charge transfer from the Pt(II) complex to the surface of TiO2. Consequently, this leads to faster electron injection in the other two nanohybrid system compared to the GQD/Pt(II):NO2@TiO2 nanohybrid.

The recombination rates of GQD/Pt(II):NO2@TiO2 and GQD/Pt(II):CHO@TiO2 nanohybrids are faster than those of other nanohybrid systems. This is because the HOMOs of Pt(II)/NO2 and Pt(II)/CHO are located on the two 4-ethynylstilbene ligands. One of the 4-ethynylstilbene ligands is positioned closer to the surface of TiO2, facilitating recombination. In contrast, the HOMOs of other Pt(II) complexes in the nanohybrid systems are primarily located on the 4-ethynylstilbene ligand that is further away from the TiO2 surface, reducing electronic coupling. Overall, the GQD/Pt(II):H and GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 demonstrate relatively faster electron injection and slower recombination rates compared to other nanocomposites, thereby favoring energy conversion.

Charge injection from the LUMO of the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite to the CBM of TiO2, as well as electron-hole recombination from the TiO2 CBM to the nanocomposite HOMO, can significantly affect the short-circuit photocurrent (Jsc) of the photocatalytic system. More efficient and faster charge injection results in a higher transfer of electrons to the TiO2 conduction band, leading to a higher short-circuit photocurrent by generating more photocurrent. In contrast, electron-hole recombination removes electrons from contributing to the photocurrent, resulting in a lower short-circuit photocurrent. Slower recombination allows electrons more time to flow through the external circuit before recombining, thereby producing a higher short-circuit photocurrent. Ultimately, the balance between charge injection efficiency and kinetics, which tend to increase the short-circuit photocurrent, versus electron-hole recombination rates, which tend to reduce the short-circuit photocurrent, determines the magnitude of the short-circuit photocurrent generated by this proposed GQD/Pt(II)-TiO2 hybrid photocatalytic system. The calculated energy conversion efficiencies (Ƞ) are as follows: GQD/Pt(II):CHO (9.49%) > GQD/Pt(II):OCH3 (8.85%) > GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 (8.72%) > GQD/Pt(II):Br (8.57%) > GQD/Pt(II):H@ (7.55%) > GQD/Pt(II):NO2 (4.71%). The GQD/Pt(II):NO2 has the smallest Ƞ among all nanocomposites due to its smallest short-circuit photocurrent and open-circuit voltage. The former is caused by relatively faster electron-hole recombination times, while the latter is due to the shorter excited-state electron lifetime. It should be noted that since the electron injection time is in the femtosecond range, which is orders of magnitude shorter than the electron relaxation time, the calculated injection efficiency (supplemental equation (3)) approaches unity, suggesting that the electron injection time does not play a dominant role in energy conversion efficiency. On the contrary, the GQD/Pt(II):CHO has the largest energy conversion efficiency among all nanohybrids, despite its fast electron-hole recombination time, second only to GQD/Pt(II):NO2. A closer inspection of the optical spectra suggests that there are more states with high oscillator strengths in GQD/Pt(II):NO2 than in the other nanocomposites, resulting in greater light collection efficiency and thus boosting the short-circuit current. The remaining nanocomposites have energy conversion efficiencies varying between 7.55% and 8.85%. Although the electron-hole recombination time of GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 is faster than that of GQD/Pt(II):Br@TiO2, it has a greater number of excited states with high oscillator strengths, which give rise to a larger short-circuit current and, consequently, larger energy conversion efficiency.

The photocatalytic performance of the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite is heavily influenced by its first-order hyperpolarizability (κ). This property determines the material's response to an electric field, impacting its light absorption, charge transfer processes, and structural stability. A higher first-order polarizability enhances light absorption, increasing the number of absorbed photons and promoting electron excitation and charge separation. Additionally, it improves the charge transfer kinetics within the nanocomposite, facilitating efficient electron transfer between GQD and Pt(II) complex. This enhanced charge separation and transfer contribute to the nanocomposite's improved photocatalytic activity. Moreover, a higher polarizability ensures better structural stability, minimizing undesired charge recombination and maintaining long-lived charge-separated states. Therefore, optimizing the first-order polarizability of the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite is crucial for designing highly efficient photocatalytic materials with applications in energy conversion and environmental remediation.

When comparing the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites, the κ values vary based on the auxiliary group connected to the Pt(II) complex. The GQD/Pt(II) nanocomposites with different auxiliary groups exhibit the following κ values: GQD/Pt(II):NO2 has the highest κ value of 2901, followed by GQD/Pt(II):CHO with a κ value of 1716. The κ values decrease further for GQD/Pt(II):OCH3 (826), GQD/Pt(II):Br (804), GQD/Pt(II):NPh2 (723), and GQD/Pt(II):H (470). Likewise, when examining the Pt(II) complexes without GQD, variations in κ values are observed. Pt(II)–CHO has the highest κ value of 1703, followed by Pt(II):Br (1040) and Pt(II):NPh2 (1259). The κ values decrease further for Pt(II):OCH3 (997), Pt(II)-H (623), and Pt(II)–NO2 (561). Specifically, when the auxiliary group attached to the Pt(II) complex possesses electron-donating ability or weak electron-withdrawing ability, the incorporation of GQD reduces the hyperpolarizability. This indicates that the presence of GQD along with such auxiliary groups dampens the overall hyperpolarizability of the nanocomposite. The electronic interactions between GQD and the auxiliary group may alter the charge distribution, resulting in a decreased ability of the material to generate or respond to an electric field. Conversely, when the auxiliary group attached to the Pt(II) complex exhibits strong electron-withdrawing ability, the inclusion of GQD increases the hyperpolarizability. This suggests that combining GQD with such auxiliary groups enhances the material's capability to generate or respond to an electric field. The presence of GQD may introduce additional charge transfer pathways or modify the electronic structure, leading to an enhancement in the material's nonlinear optical response.

In general, the GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposite with the CHO as an auxiliary group exhibits a relatively larger short-circuit current and a first-order hyperpolarizability. Therefore, it is more suitable for use in photocatalytic applications than the other molecular systems.

This computational work provides critical guidance for optimizing solar energy conversion efficiency in GQD/Pt(II) complex nanocomposites through strategic substituent selection. The modeling gives insights into the complex interplay between structure, optoelectronic properties, and photoconversion performance.

The computations show substituents significantly impact light absorption profiles by altering the nanocomposite electronic structure and transition dipole moments. This directly affects the number of photons harvested. Substituents also modify charge carrier separation and transport by changing charge transfer configurations and interfacial electronic coupling. This impacts collection efficiencies. Additionally, substituents tune the band energetics and interfacial electrochemistry, altering thermodynamic driving forces for surface reduction–oxidation reactions. This affects regenerative kinetics and electrical outputs. Furthermore, the GQD component introduces additional pathways for charge separation and delocalization. This enhances the substituent effects on conversion efficiency. Optimizing substituents involves balancing often competing effects on light absorption, charge dynamics, and electrochemical surface reactions.

Overall, the modeling provides guidance for substituent selection to maximize photon absorption, charge generation and transport, and surface catalytic activity. By computationally linking molecular structure to conversion performance, this work establishes design rules and principles for engineering nanocomposite photocatalysts with superior photovoltaic capabilities. The framework paves the way for in silico optimization of next-generation solar energy harvesting materials.

Peng Cui: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qiulan Wu: Funding acquisition.
